The evolution of GIS in conservation

From the first digital map to real-time global monitoring

← scroll to explore →
1964
The first GIS — Canada Geographic Information System
Roger Tomlinson built CGIS for Canadian land-use planning — the first operational GIS ever created. It introduced spatial overlay as a decision tool. Only large governments could access it, but it became the foundation for everything that followed.
CGISTomlinsonSpatial overlay
Longley et al., 2015
1980s – 90s
ArcGIS + Landsat — conservation goes digital
Esri's ArcGIS made spatial modeling commercially accessible. NASA Landsat opened its archive, enabling long-term monitoring of habitat loss and land change at regional and global scales.
ArcGISEsri/DangermondNASA Landsat
Wulder et al., 2012
2000s
Landscape scale — ecosystems, gap analysis, global databases
USGS published national land cover maps. Gap analysis identified underrepresented ecosystems. IUCN and Protected Planet launched the WDPA. Goodchild's research defined GIS science as a discipline.
USGS NLCDIUCN/WDPAGoodchild
USGS, 2023 · UNEP-WCMC & IUCN, 2023
2010s
Rise of citizen platforms and open biodiversity data
Smartphones and cloud computing turned the public into data contributors. iNaturalist and GBIF generated research-grade species observations at unprecedented scale, giving conservation GIS a live global data layer.
iNaturalistGBIFCitizen science
Campbell et al., 2023
Today
Real-time monitoring — drones, LiDAR, machine learning
Drone imagery, LiDAR, and ML classification enable centimeter-resolution habitat mapping. Real-time cloud platforms connect agencies worldwide — all built on the same idea Tomlinson introduced in 1964.
Drone imageryLiDARML classification
Turner et al., 2015
